A journal is analyzed. From it, an extraction procedure produces object-concepts and perhaps a count like 289 unique words. But if that extraction reflects the model’s preferred way of carving up the text, then it does not reliably reveal the causal structure Einstein actually used. So the extracted units may be descriptive of the analyzer, not of the invention.

Now suppose the operative units of search were not words at all. Instead, they were constrained schemas: observer, signal, frame, simultaneity, contradiction. These are not arbitrary tokens but linked roles in a thought experiment. If search proceeds by arranging and testing such schemas, then the relevant combinatorial space is not the enormous one suggested by free recombination over many lexical items. It is a much smaller space, because the schemas are constrained by compatibility and by the requirement that they form intelligible tests.

Under those conditions, the figure based on 289 unique words loses force. Even if true, it may not track novelty. Relativity could have arisen almost entirely from new relations among familiar concepts: how an observer in one frame judges simultaneity using signals, and how contradiction appears if one assumes incompatible constraints. Then lexical variety is incidental. A sparse vocabulary could still support a decisive conceptual reorganization.

So the thought experiment shifts. Imagine two descriptions of the same journal. One description lists words or extracted concept-objects and estimates a huge possibility space. The other description lists a handful of tightly constrained schemas and the legal relations among them. If the first decomposition is partly an artifact of the model, while the second is closer to the actual causal machinery, then the first overstates the difficulty of the search by counting distinctions that did no real work. The second better explains how novelty can emerge from ordinary terms.

The final two premises introduce strings that do not obviously join the schema set: “Unreposefulness sterlings hornfels preenact cerin” and “Droughtiness therence eole cruddle reams.” Given the earlier premises, their presence reinforces the point. Mere word uniqueness cannot by itself indicate conceptual importance. These strings may increase lexical diversity while contributing nothing to the relevant relational structure. If novelty lives in the organization of observer, signal, frame, simultaneity, and contradiction, then unfamiliar words are evidentially weak unless they enter that structure.

What follows is modest but sharp: from the journal alone, one cannot infer the true size or nature of Einstein’s search space simply from extracted object-concepts or word counts. If the causal engine was a compact set of constrained thought-experiment schemas, then the effective space was smaller, the lexical count was largely irrelevant, and decomposition-based estimates risk measuring the analyst’s ontology rather than the inventor’s reasoning.
